rationale_for_baseline_tier: Uncomplicated tinea corporis (ringworm): a characteristic, video-assessable annular scaly rash with central clearing, a clear zoonotic trigger (new kitten), aggravation by warmth/humidity, and a negative screen for systemic upset, secondary infection, scalp involvement and mucosal lesions. This is a self-limiting fungal infection safely managed with an over-the-counter topical antifungal and infection-control measures; a low-touch tier (T1 — routine GP/pharmacist-manageable, self-care/OTC primary) with robust safety-netting is correct. The rapid spread and contagion anxiety do not raise the true acuity: over-triage to urgent same-day in-person review, ED or 000 for the current benign presentation would be a calibration error. Because the condition does need a specific antifungal (pharmacist-suppliable), the minimum viable tier for a pass is T1 rather than pure self-care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin becomes hot and intensely painful, develops fluid-filled blisters or pus, or spreading hot redness with fever (secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis)
escalation_edges.rationale: These features indicate secondary bacterial infection or cellulitis, which needs prompt assessment and antibiotics; systemic upset with fever raises the urgency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin becomes hot and very painful, develops blisters or pus, or the redness spreads and you get a fever, see a doctor the same day (urgent care or GP) — it may have become infected and need antibiotics.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash spreads to the scalp, or a boggy, painful, swollen lump develops within the rash (tinea capitis / kerion)
escalation_edges.rationale: Scalp involvement or a kerion needs ORAL antifungal therapy (creams alone will not clear it) and, if untreated, can cause scarring hair loss — it warrants prompt GP review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash spreads onto your scalp, or you develop a boggy, painful, swollen lump in the rash, see your GP within a few days — that type needs antifungal tablets rather than just cream.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rash is not clearly receding after about a week of correct antifungal treatment, or continues to spread
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: About 1 week, or sooner if spreading
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure to respond to appropriate antifungal therapy warrants GP review to reconsider the diagnosis (a mimic such as secondary syphilis or eczema), confirm with a KOH scraping, or step up to oral therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it is not clearly getting better after about a week of using the cream properly, or it keeps spreading, see your GP — they can confirm the diagnosis with a simple skin scraping and change the treatment if needed.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Household with young children and a new kitten; high patient concern about transmission
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise the infection-control and source-control measures (dedicated hot-washed towel, no sharing of towels/bedding, keeping the area covered where practical, vet review of the kitten, and checking the children for any new rings) — these both reassure the anxious parent and genuinely reduce household spread. Escalate a child promptly if they develop scalp involvement.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You can safely treat this at home. Get an over-the-counter antifungal cream (such as terbinafine or clotrimazole) from a pharmacist and apply it to the rings and a couple of centimetres of normal skin around them twice a day, continuing for the full course even after the skin looks clear. Use a separate towel for the area and hot-wash it after every use, don't share towels or bedding, keep the skin dry, and have the kitten checked by a vet. The itch usually eases within a few days and the rash clears over 1-2 weeks.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with a pharmacist-supplied antifungal. For non-urgent advice, call your regular GP or 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84, 24/7). See a GP the same day if the skin becomes hot, painful, blistered or you develop a fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for non-urgent questions use 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) or an after-hours telehealth service; seek same-day care if the skin becomes hot, intensely painful, blistered or you develop a fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: If you do see your GP, mention when the rash started, the new kitten, how it has spread, that a moisturiser didn't help, and what antifungal you have been using and for how long.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: For this benign fungal infection the baseline itself carries no critical under-triage risk (topical/self-care is safe). The genuine failure is giving advice without any antifungal or without safety-netting — e.g. reassuring as 'just a rash' while omitting treatment of the infection and the warnings about secondary infection, scalp involvement or non-response.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient is reassured with no effective antifungal and no safety-netting; the infection spreads to the children and scalp, a kerion develops unnoticed, or a secondary cellulitis is not acted on.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an uncomplicated, currently benign ringworm to urgent care, the emergency department, or calling 000 — over-triage that wastes acute resources and causes unnecessary alarm for a condition a pharmacist can manage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Mycological confirmation (KOH microscopy/culture) is not obtainable in telehealth, and rare annular-rash mimics (e.g. secondary syphilis) cannot be fully excluded by chat — but the classic morphology, zoonotic exposure and negative systemic/mucosal screen make uncomplicated tinea corporis overwhelmingly likely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Start appropriate topical antifungal treatment with infection-control advice and give explicit, time-bound safety-netting for secondary infection, scalp involvement and non-response, asking the patient to see a GP for a confirmatory scraping if it does not respond — rather than either over-escalating a benign infection now or delaying treatment for confirmation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either over-triaging an uncomplicated ringworm to ED/000 or insisting on in-person review before any treatment, or reassuring with no antifungal and no safety-net for the complications and mimics.
